tool_input.prompt:
Create the file /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json

This is a comprehensive nonprofit data type taxonomy with ~120 data types across 10 categories, organized into 4 classification tiers.

**Tiers:**
- T1 "Public" — Intended for public consumption; no harm if disclosed
- T2 "Internal" — For staff/authorized volunteers; low harm if accidentally disclosed  
- T3 "Confidential" — Sensitive organizational or personal data; moderate-to-significant harm if disclosed
- T4 "Restricted" — Highest sensitivity; legal, regulatory, or severe harm if disclosed

**Structure:**
```json
{
  "tiers": {
    "T1": { "id": "T1", "label": "Public", "color": "#16a34a", "bgColor": "#f0fdf4", "borderColor": "#bbf7d0", "description": "...", "riskIfDisclosed": "None", "accessDefault": "Anyone" },
    "T2": { "id": "T2", "label": "Internal", "color": "#2563eb", "bgColor": "#eff6ff", "borderColor": "#bfdbfe", "description": "...", "riskIfDisclosed": "Low", "accessDefault": "All staff with a need" },
    "T3": { "id": "T3", "label": "Confidential", "color": "#d97706", "bgColor": "#fffbeb", "borderColor": "#fde68a", "description": "...", "riskIfDisclosed": "Moderate to High", "accessDefault": "Role-based, need-to-know" },
    "T4": { "id": "T4", "label": "Restricted", "color": "#dc2626", "bgColor": "#fef2f2", "borderColor": "#fecaca", "description": "...", "riskIfDisclosed": "Severe", "accessDefault": "Explicit authorization only" }
  },
  "categories": [
    {
      "id": "donor-funder",
      "name": "Donor & Funder Data",
      "icon": "heart",
      "description": "Information related to individual donors, institutional funders, and fundraising activities",
      "dataTypes": [
        {
          "name": "Published donor lists (annual report, honor roll)",
          "tier": "T1",
          "rationale": "Explicitly published with donor consent",
          "regulations": [],
          "sectors": ["all"]
        },
        ...
      ]
    },
    ...
  ]
}
```

**Categories and their data types (be comprehensive — aim for ~12 per category):**

1. **Donor & Funder Data** — Published donor lists (T1), fundraising totals (T1), donor name + giving total (T3), donor contact info (T3), donor payment/credit card/ACH data (T4), wealth screening/capacity scores (T3), donor communication preferences (T3), major gift solicitation strategies (T3), planned giving/estate docs (T4), 501(c)(4) donor identity/Schedule B (T4), lapsed donor reason codes (T3), funder LOI and grant agreements (T3), funder relationship notes (T3), anonymous donor records (T3)

2. **Program Participant / Client / Beneficiary Data** — Aggregated program outcomes (T1), program descriptions (T1), client name and contact (T3), client intake forms (T3), client SSN/government ID (T4), client immigration status (T4), client mental health records (T4), client substance use records (T4), client HIV/AIDS status (T4), client DV/shelter location (T4), HMIS client records (T4), client financial data (T3), client case notes (T3), client legal records (T4), client benefit enrollment (T3), client education records (T3), client photos/video identifiable (T3), de-identified participant data (T2), research data with participant PII (T4)

3. **Employee & HR Data** — Staff directory name/title/email (T2), published leadership bios (T1), employee SSN (T4), employee DOB (T3), employee home address/personal contact (T3), payroll and compensation (T4), performance reviews and disciplinary records (T3), medical/disability accommodation records (T4), background check results (T4), I-9 immigration verification (T4), EEO demographic data (T3), benefit enrollment and dependent info (T3), termination docs and severance (T3), workplace incident reports (T4)

4. **Financial Data** — Published Form 990 (T1), audited financial statements published (T1), annual budget board-approved (T2), detailed line-item budget with variances (T3), chart of accounts and GL detail (T3), vendor contracts and pricing (T3), banking account numbers/routing (T4), investment accounts and portfolio (T3), endowment details restricted funds (T3), payroll runs individual compensation (T4), AP/AR detail (T3), grant financial reports (T3), tax filings beyond 990 (T3), credit card/purchasing card data (T4)

5. **Legal & Governance Data** — Articles of incorporation (T1), IRS determination letter (T1), board member names/bios published (T1), board meeting minutes public portions (T2), executive session board minutes (T4), bylaws (T2), conflict of interest disclosures (T3), litigation files and legal correspondence (T4), contracts vendor/partner/funder (T3), insurance policies (T3), regulatory filings state charity (T2), whistleblower complaints (T4), IP filings/trademarks (T3), merger/dissolution documents (T4)

6. **Communications & Advocacy Data** — Published press releases (T1), marketing materials (T1), social media content (T1), email newsletter subscriber list (T3), advocacy contact lists (T3), lobbying contact records (T3), advocacy strategy documents (T3), draft policy positions (T2), coalition partner lists (T2), media contact lists (T2), crisis communications plans (T3), social media account credentials (T4)

7. **Grant & Proposal Data** — Published grant awards (T1), program narratives submitted (T2), pending grant proposals (T3), grant budgets submitted (T3), grant reports to funders (T3), funder prospect research (T3), declined proposal and funder feedback (T3), grant agreement/contract terms (T3), subaward/subcontract agreements (T3), federal grant compliance docs (T3), proprietary program models/logic models (T3), human subjects research protocols IRB (T4)

8. **Volunteer Data** — Volunteer roster name/role internal (T2), volunteer contact information (T3), volunteer application and screening (T3), volunteer background check results (T4), volunteer hours and service logs (T2), volunteer reference letters (T3), minor volunteer records (T4), volunteer certifications/training (T2), volunteer emergency contact info (T3)

9. **Case Management Data** — Case numbers/client IDs non-identifying (T2), case intake records (T3), service plans and goal-setting (T3), progress notes and case narrative (T3), crisis intervention records (T4), safety plans DV/suicide/child abuse (T4), referral records (T3), client consent/release forms (T3), case closure records (T3), child welfare/mandated reporting records (T4), housing placement records (T3), benefits navigation records (T3)

10. **Research Data** — Published research and reports (T1), survey data aggregated de-identified (T1), survey data individual identified (T3), focus group/interview transcripts identified (T3), human subjects research data with PII (T4), proprietary evaluation frameworks (T3), unpublished research findings (T3), vendor/tool evaluation data (T2), program evaluation contracts (T3)

**Regulations array values:** Use these string constants: "HIPAA", "FERPA", "HMIS", "PCI-DSS", "GDPR", "STATE_PRIVACY", "42_CFR_PART_2", "COPPA", "FCRA", "ADA", "VAWA", "CAN-SPAM", "NLRA", "SOX"

**Sectors array values:** "all", "health", "education", "housing", "advocacy", "arts", "human_services", "environment"

Write the complete, valid JSON file. Make sure every data type has all fields: name, tier, rationale, regulations (array), sectors (array).

Create TWO data files for the nonprofit data classification web app.

**File 1: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/data/handling-matrix.json**

This defines the data handling controls required for each of the 4 classification tiers. Structure:

```json
{
  "controls": [
    {
      "id": "nda",
      "category": "Sharing",
      "name": "NDA Requirement",
      "description": "Non-disclosure agreement requirement for external sharing",
      "tiers": {
        "T1": { "requirement": "none", "label": "Not required", "detail": "Information is public" },
        "T2": { "requirement": "none", "label": "Not required", "detail": "Use professional judgment when sharing externally" },
        "T3": { "requirement": "recommended", "label": "Recommended", "detail": "NDA recommended before sharing with external parties" },
        "T4": { "requirement": "required", "label": "Required", "detail": "NDA must be executed before any external access" }
      }
    },
    ...
  ]
}
```

Include these controls with appropriate tier requirements:
1. NDA Requirement (Sharing)
2. Encryption in Transit (Network)
3. Encryption at Rest (Storage)
4. Access Control (Access) — who can access
5. Mobile Device Controls (Devices)
6. Email Handling (Communication)
7. AI Tool Usage (Technology) — critical: T4 and T3 should prohibit unless on approved list, T2 should be caution, T1 no restriction
8. Physical Mail (Physical)
9. Instant Messaging (Communication) — generally prohibited for T3/T4
10. Remote Access (Network) — VPN/2FA requirements
11. Data Disposal (Lifecycle) — secure deletion requirements
12. Labeling (Documentation) — how to mark/label documents
13. Printing (Physical) — restrictions on printing sensitive docs
14. Cloud Storage (Storage) — approved platforms only for T3/T4
15. Third-Party Sharing (Sharing) — requirements for sharing with vendors/partners

For each control, the requirement field should be one of: "none", "optional", "recommended", "required", "prohibited"

**File 2: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/data/workspace-features.json**

Google Workspace features by tier for nonprofits:

```json
{
  "tiers": [
    {
      "id": "free",
      "name": "Nonprofits (Free)",
      "equivalent": "Business Starter",
      "pricePerUser": "$0",
      "features": {

        "allowlistedDomains": { "available": true, "label": "Basic", "detail": "Can allowlist specific domains for sharing" },
        "sharedDriveControls": { "available": false, "label": "Limited", "detail": "Cannot restrict sharing per Shared Drive" },
        "targetAudiences": { "available": false, "label": "Not available", "detail": "Custom sharing groups require Business Standard" },
        "trustRules": { "available": false, "label": "Not available", "detail": "Granular per-user sharing rules require Enterprise" },
        "gmailConfidential": { "available": true, "label": "Available", "detail": "Set expiration, prevent forwarding/copying. Not true encryption." },
        "irmViewerRestrict": { "available": true, "label": "Available", "detail": "File owners can restrict viewers from downloading/printing/copying" },
        "irmAdminEnforced": { "available": false, "label": "Not available", "detail": "Admin-enforced IRM via DLP requires Enterprise" },
        "vault": { "available": false, "label": "Not available", "detail": "eDiscovery and retention requires Business Plus" },
        "contextAwareAccess": { "available": false, "label": "Not available", "detail": "Location/device-based access rules require Enterprise" },
        "securityCenter": { "available": false, "label": "Not available", "detail": "Security analytics dashboard requires Enterprise" }
      },
      "recommendations": [
        "Set domain-wide sharing to 'Only people in your organization' as default",
        "Use Gmail Confidential Mode for sensitive external emails",
        "Train staff to manually restrict download/print on sensitive files",
        "Use Shared Drive structure with team-based access for basic access control",
        "Consider upgrading to Business Standard ($3.50/user/mo) for Drive labels and Shared Drive controls"
      ]
    },
    ...add Business Standard, Business Plus, Enterprise Standard tiers with their features and recommendations
  ],
  "implementationChecklist": {
    "free": [
      { "step": "Configure domain-wide sharing restrictions", "path": "Admin Console > Apps > Google Workspace > Drive > Sharing settings", "detail": "Set default to 'Only people in your organization can access'" },
      { "step": "Enable Gmail Confidential Mode", "path": "Admin Console > Apps > Google Workspace > Gmail > User settings", "detail": "Ensure confidential mode is enabled for all users" },
      { "step": "Set up Shared Drives with team access", "path": "Google Drive > Shared Drives > Create", "detail": "Create drives per department/function. Note: sharing controls are limited on free tier" },
      { "step": "Enable 2-Step Verification", "path": "Admin Console > Security > Authentication > 2-Step Verification", "detail": "Enforce for all users. Use security keys if possible." },
      { "step": "Configure mobile device management", "path": "Admin Console > Devices > Mobile & endpoints", "detail": "Require screen lock, enable remote wipe" },
      { "step": "Review third-party app access", "path": "Admin Console > Security > API Controls > App access control", "detail": "Block or restrict untrusted third-party apps" },
      { "step": "Train staff on manual file protections", "path": "N/A — training activity", "detail": "Show staff how to restrict download/print/copy on sensitive files via the Share dialog" }
    ],
    ...add checklists for businessStandard, businessPlus, enterprise
  }
}
```

For Business Standard ($3.50/user/mo for nonprofits): add Drive labels (manual), Gmail labels (manual), per-Shared Drive controls, target audiences. Still no DLP, trust rules, vault, or security center.

For Business Plus ($5.04/user/mo): add Vault (eDiscovery/retention). Still no DLP, trust rules, admin IRM, security center.

For Enterprise Standard: add everything — DLP, auto-labeling, trust rules, admin-enforced IRM, context-aware access, security center, S/MIME.

Make sure the recommendations are practical and actionable for each tier. Write both complete JSON files.

      { "step": "Create target audiences", "path": "Admin Console > Directory > Target audiences", "detail": "Create audiences for departments (HR, Finance, Programs) to control sharing suggestions and defaults" },
      { "step": "Configure Shared Drive access levels", "path": "Google Drive > Shared Drive > Manage members", "detail": "Review membership of each Shared Drive; remove unnecessary access; use Content Manager or Contributor roles instead of Manager where possible" },
      { "step": "Train staff on label usage", "path": "N/A -- training activity", "detail": "Train all staff to apply appropriate classification labels when creating, uploading, or modifying files" },
      { "step": "Set up sharing audit process", "path": "Admin Console > Reports > Apps reports > Drive", "detail": "Schedule monthly review of external sharing activity to catch drift" }
    ],
    "businessPlus": [
      { "step": "All Business Standard steps above", "path": "See Business Standard checklist", "detail": "Complete all foundational and Standard-tier steps first" },
      { "step": "Enable Google Vault", "path": "Admin Console > Apps > Google Workspace > Google Vault", "detail": "Activate Vault and assign Vault privileges to compliance/legal staff" },
      { "step": "Create retention rules", "path": "Vault > Retention > Custom rules", "detail": "Set retention periods aligned with your data retention policy: e.g., 7 years for financial records, 3 years for program data, indefinite for legal holds" },
      { "step": "Configure default retention", "path": "Vault > Retention > Default rule", "detail": "Set a default retention period to prevent premature deletion of data that may be needed" },
      { "step": "Set up legal holds", "path": "Vault > Matters > Create matter", "detail": "Create matters for any active litigation, investigation, or regulatory inquiry; add relevant custodians" },
      { "step": "Train compliance staff on Vault", "path": "N/A -- training activity", "detail": "Ensure at least 2 staff members know how to search, export, and manage Vault data" },
      { "step": "Document Vault procedures", "path": "N/A -- documentation", "detail": "Create an internal procedure for legal hold requests, data exports, and retention rule changes" },
      { "step": "Audit existing data", "path": "Vault > Search", "detail": "Run initial searches to understand what data exists and verify retention rules are capturing expected content" }
    ],
    "enterprise": [
      { "step": "All Business Plus steps above", "path": "See Business Plus checklist", "detail": "Complete all foundational, Standard, and Plus-tier steps first" },
      { "step": "Design DLP rules", "path": "Admin Console > Security > Data Protection > Manage rules", "detail": "Create rules for common sensitive data: SSNs (XXX-XX-XXXX), credit card numbers, health record identifiers. Start in 'Audit only' mode before enforcing." },
      { "step": "Configure DLP auto-labeling", "path": "Admin Console > Security > Data Protection > Manage rules", "detail": "Set DLP rules to automatically apply classification labels when sensitive content is detected in Drive or Gmail" },
      { "step": "Set up trust rules", "path": "Admin Console > Apps > Google Workspace > Drive > Trust rules", "detail": "Configure per-OU sharing restrictions: block HR and Finance from external sharing; allow Programs to share with allowlisted partner domains only" },
      { "step": "Enable context-aware access", "path": "Admin Console > Security > Context-Aware Access", "detail": "Create access levels based on device management status, IP range, and device encryption. Apply to T4 data applications." },
      { "step": "Configure admin-enforced IRM", "path": "Admin Console > Security > Data Protection > Manage rules", "detail": "Create DLP rules that automatically disable download/print/copy on files detected as T3/T4" },
      { "step": "Deploy S/MIME", "path": "Admin Console > Apps > Google Workspace > Gmail > User settings > S/MIME", "detail": "Enable S/MIME and distribute certificates to staff handling T4 data externally (legal, HR, executive team)" },
      { "step": "Configure Security Center", "path": "Admin Console > Security > Security Center", "detail": "Review security health page; address critical recommendations; set up custom alerts for unusual sharing activity" },
      { "step": "Create DLP incident workflow", "path": "Admin Console > Security > Data Protection > Manage rules", "detail": "Configure DLP alerts to notify security team; establish triage process for DLP incidents; document escalation path" },
      { "step": "Run DLP in audit mode first", "path": "Admin Console > Security > Data Protection > Manage rules", "detail": "Run all new DLP rules in audit-only mode for 2-4 weeks; review logs; tune rules to reduce false positives before enforcing" },
      { "step": "Schedule quarterly access reviews", "path": "N/A -- process", "detail": "Use Security Center and Drive audit logs to review who has access to T3/T4 data stores quarterly" },
      { "step": "Document DLP exceptions process", "path": "N/A -- documentation", "detail": "Create a formal process for requesting DLP exceptions with approval chain and time-limited waivers" }
    ]
  }
}

      { "step": "Set up sharing audit process", "path": "Admin Console > Reports > Apps reports > Drive", "detail": "Schedule monthly review of external sharing activity to catch drift" }

- AI Tool Usage (T3/T4): "prohibited" unless on approved tool list with signed DPA/BAA
- Instant Messaging (T3/T4): prohibited for sensitive data
- Requirements escalate from "none" at T1 to "required" or "prohibited" at T4

**`/Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/data/workspace-features.json`** -- 4 Google Workspace tiers with 15 features each, plus implementation checklists:

- **Business Standard ($3.50/user)**: Adds Drive/Gmail labels (manual), per-Shared Drive controls, target audiences. 8 additional steps.
- **Business Plus ($5.04/user)**: Adds Vault for eDiscovery/retention. 8 additional steps.
- **Enterprise Standard**: Full suite -- DLP with auto-labeling, trust rules, admin-enforced IRM, context-aware access, Security Center, S/MIME. 12 additional steps including a "run DLP in audit mode first" step to avoid disruption.

content.text:
Both files are created:

**`/Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/data/handling-matrix.json`** -- 15 controls across 7 categories (Sharing, Network, Storage, Access, Devices, Communication, Technology, Physical, Lifecycle, Documentation), each with T1-T4 tier requirements. Key highlights:
- AI Tool Usage (T3/T4): "prohibited" unless on approved tool list with signed DPA/BAA
- Instant Messaging (T3/T4): prohibited for sensitive data
- Requirements escalate from "none" at T1 to "required" or "prohibited" at T4

**`/Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/data/workspace-features.json`** -- 4 Google Workspace tiers with 15 features each, plus implementation checklists:
- **Free ($0)**: Domain sharing, Gmail Confidential, viewer IRM only. 10 checklist steps.
- **Business Standard ($3.50/user)**: Adds Drive/Gmail labels (manual), per-Shared Drive controls, target audiences. 8 additional steps.
- **Business Plus ($5.04/user)**: Adds Vault for eDiscovery/retention. 8 additional steps.
- **Enterprise Standard**: Full suite -- DLP with auto-labeling, trust rules, admin-enforced IRM, context-aware access, Security Center, S/MIME. 12 additional steps including a "run DLP in audit mode first" step to avoid disruption.

{
  "tiers": {
    "T1": {
      "id": "T1",
      "label": "Public",
      "color": "#16a34a",
      "bgColor": "#f0fdf4",
      "borderColor": "#bbf7d0",
      "description": "Information intended for public consumption. No harm if disclosed; may already be published or required to be publicly available.",
      "riskIfDisclosed": "None",
      "accessDefault": "Anyone"
    },
    "T2": {
      "id": "T2",
      "label": "Internal",
      "color": "#2563eb",
      "bgColor": "#eff6ff",
      "borderColor": "#bfdbfe",
      "description": "Operational information for staff and authorized volunteers. Low harm if accidentally disclosed, but not intended for public release.",
      "riskIfDisclosed": "Low",
      "accessDefault": "All staff with a need"
    },
    "T3": {
      "id": "T3",
      "label": "Confidential",
      "color": "#d97706",
      "bgColor": "#fffbeb",
      "borderColor": "#fde68a",
      "description": "Sensitive organizational or personal data. Moderate-to-significant harm if disclosed, including reputational damage, loss of donor trust, or regulatory concern.",
      "riskIfDisclosed": "Moderate to High",
      "accessDefault": "Role-based, need-to-know"
    },
    "T4": {
      "id": "T4",
      "label": "Restricted",
      "color": "#dc2626",
      "bgColor": "#fef2f2",
      "borderColor": "#fecaca",
      "description": "Highest sensitivity data. Disclosure could cause severe harm including legal liability, regulatory penalties, threat to personal safety, or identity theft.",
      "riskIfDisclosed": "Severe",
      "accessDefault": "Explicit authorization only"
    }
  },
  "categories": [

tool_input.command:
python3 -c "
import json
with open('src/data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json') as f:
    data = json.load(f)

total = 0
for cat in data['categories']:
    count = len(cat['dataTypes'])
    total += count
    print(f\"  {cat['name']}: {count} data types\")
    for dt in cat['dataTypes']:
        # Validate all fields exist
        for field in ['name','tier','rationale','regulations','sectors']:
            assert field in dt, f'Missing {field} in {dt[\"name\"]}'
        assert dt['tier'] in ['T1','T2','T3','T4'], f'Bad tier in {dt[\"name\"]}'
        assert isinstance(dt['regulations'], list), f'regulations not list in {dt[\"name\"]}'
        assert isinstance(dt['sectors'], list), f'sectors not list in {dt[\"name\"]}'

print(f'\nTotal data types: {total}')
print(f'Categories: {len(data[\"categories\"])}')
print(f'Tiers: {len(data[\"tiers\"])}')
print('Validation: PASSED')
"

tool_input.prompt:
Create the file /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json

This is a comprehensive nonprofit data type taxonomy with ~120 data types across 10 categories, organized into 4 classification tiers.

**Tiers:**
- T1 "Public" — Intended for public consumption; no harm if disclosed
- T2 "Internal" — For staff/authorized volunteers; low harm if accidentally disclosed  
- T3 "Confidential" — Sensitive organizational or personal data; moderate-to-significant harm if disclosed
- T4 "Restricted" — Highest sensitivity; legal, regulatory, or severe harm if disclosed

**Structure:**
```json
{
  "tiers": {
    "T1": { "id": "T1", "label": "Public", "color": "#16a34a", "bgColor": "#f0fdf4", "borderColor": "#bbf7d0", "description": "...", "riskIfDisclosed": "None", "accessDefault": "Anyone" },
    "T2": { "id": "T2", "label": "Internal", "color": "#2563eb", "bgColor": "#eff6ff", "borderColor": "#bfdbfe", "description": "...", "riskIfDisclosed": "Low", "accessDefault": "All staff with a need" },
    "T3": { "id": "T3", "label": "Confidential", "color": "#d97706", "bgColor": "#fffbeb", "borderColor": "#fde68a", "description": "...", "riskIfDisclosed": "Moderate to High", "accessDefault": "Role-based, need-to-know" },
    "T4": { "id": "T4", "label": "Restricted", "color": "#dc2626", "bgColor": "#fef2f2", "borderColor": "#fecaca", "description": "...", "riskIfDisclosed": "Severe", "accessDefault": "Explicit authorization only" }
  },
  "categories": [
    {
      "id": "donor-funder",
      "name": "Donor & Funder Data",
      "icon": "heart",
      "description": "Information related to individual donors, institutional funders, and fundraising activities",
      "dataTypes": [
        {
          "name": "Published donor lists (annual report, honor roll)",
          "tier": "T1",
          "rationale": "Explicitly published with donor consent",
          "regulations": [],
          "sectors": ["all"]
        },
        ...
      ]
    },
    ...
  ]
}
```

**Categories and their data types (be comprehensive — aim for ~12 per category):**

1. **Donor & Funder Data** — Published donor lists (T1), fundraising totals (T1), donor name + giving total (T3), donor contact info (T3), donor payment/credit card/ACH data (T4), wealth screening/capacity scores (T3), donor communication preferences (T3), major gift solicitation strategies (T3), planned giving/estate docs (T4), 501(c)(4) donor identity/Schedule B (T4), lapsed donor reason codes (T3), funder LOI and grant agreements (T3), funder relationship notes (T3), anonymous donor records (T3)

2. **Program Participant / Client / Beneficiary Data** — Aggregated program outcomes (T1), program descriptions (T1), client name and contact (T3), client intake forms (T3), client SSN/government ID (T4), client immigration status (T4), client mental health records (T4), client substance use records (T4), client HIV/AIDS status (T4), client DV/shelter location (T4), HMIS client records (T4), client financial data (T3), client case notes (T3), client legal records (T4), client benefit enrollment (T3), client education records (T3), client photos/video identifiable (T3), de-identified participant data (T2), research data with participant PII (T4)

3. **Employee & HR Data** — Staff directory name/title/email (T2), published leadership bios (T1), employee SSN (T4), employee DOB (T3), employee home address/personal contact (T3), payroll and compensation (T4), performance reviews and disciplinary records (T3), medical/disability accommodation records (T4), background check results (T4), I-9 immigration verification (T4), EEO demographic data (T3), benefit enrollment and dependent info (T3), termination docs and severance (T3), workplace incident reports (T4)

4. **Financial Data** — Published Form 990 (T1), audited financial statements published (T1), annual budget board-approved (T2), detailed line-item budget with variances (T3), chart of accounts and GL detail (T3), vendor contracts and pricing (T3), banking account numbers/routing (T4), investment accounts and portfolio (T3), endowment details restricted funds (T3), payroll runs individual compensation (T4), AP/AR detail (T3), grant financial reports (T3), tax filings beyond 990 (T3), credit card/purchasing card data (T4)

5. **Legal & Governance Data** — Articles of incorporation (T1), IRS determination letter (T1), board member names/bios published (T1), board meeting minutes public portions (T2), executive session board minutes (T4), bylaws (T2), conflict of interest disclosures (T3), litigation files and legal correspondence (T4), contracts vendor/partner/funder (T3), insurance policies (T3), regulatory filings state charity (T2), whistleblower complaints (T4), IP filings/trademarks (T3), merger/dissolution documents (T4)

6. **Communications & Advocacy Data** — Published press releases (T1), marketing materials (T1), social media content (T1), email newsletter subscriber list (T3), advocacy contact lists (T3), lobbying contact records (T3), advocacy strategy documents (T3), draft policy positions (T2), coalition partner lists (T2), media contact lists (T2), crisis communications plans (T3), social media account credentials (T4)

7. **Grant & Proposal Data** — Published grant awards (T1), program narratives submitted (T2), pending grant proposals (T3), grant budgets submitted (T3), grant reports to funders (T3), funder prospect research (T3), declined proposal and funder feedback (T3), grant agreement/contract terms (T3), subaward/subcontract agreements (T3), federal grant compliance docs (T3), proprietary program models/logic models (T3), human subjects research protocols IRB (T4)

8. **Volunteer Data** — Volunteer roster name/role internal (T2), volunteer contact information (T3), volunteer application and screening (T3), volunteer background check results (T4), volunteer hours and service logs (T2), volunteer reference letters (T3), minor volunteer records (T4), volunteer certifications/training (T2), volunteer emergency contact info (T3)

9. **Case Management Data** — Case numbers/client IDs non-identifying (T2), case intake records (T3), service plans and goal-setting (T3), progress notes and case narrative (T3), crisis intervention records (T4), safety plans DV/suicide/child abuse (T4), referral records (T3), client consent/release forms (T3), case closure records (T3), child welfare/mandated reporting records (T4), housing placement records (T3), benefits navigation records (T3)

10. **Research Data** — Published research and reports (T1), survey data aggregated de-identified (T1), survey data individual identified (T3), focus group/interview transcripts identified (T3), human subjects research data with PII (T4), proprietary evaluation frameworks (T3), unpublished research findings (T3), vendor/tool evaluation data (T2), program evaluation contracts (T3)

**Regulations array values:** Use these string constants: "HIPAA", "FERPA", "HMIS", "PCI-DSS", "GDPR", "STATE_PRIVACY", "42_CFR_PART_2", "COPPA", "FCRA", "ADA", "VAWA", "CAN-SPAM", "NLRA", "SOX"

**Sectors array values:** "all", "health", "education", "housing", "advocacy", "arts", "human_services", "environment"

Write the complete, valid JSON file. Make sure every data type has all fields: name, tier, rationale, regulations (array), sectors (array).

tool_input.prompt:
Create two React page components for a nonprofit data classification web app. The app uses Tailwind CSS v3 (via @tailwindcss/vite, so use standard Tailwind classes). The design should be clean, warm, accessible — earthy greens and stone colors. Import data from the taxonomy file.

**File 1: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/Landing.jsx**

A landing page that:
- Has a hero section with a gradient background (emerald/teal) explaining what data classification is and why nonprofits need it
- "Protect Your Mission by Protecting Your Data" as the headline
- Subtitle: "A free, practical guide to classifying and handling sensitive information in your nonprofit — built for Google Workspace"
- Shows the 4 tiers as color-coded cards (green=Public, blue=Internal, amber=Confidential, red=Restricted) with brief descriptions
- Has a "Get Started" section with 3 cards linking to: Guide (learn), Decision Tree (classify), Wizard (generate policy)
- Uses Link from react-router-dom for navigation
- Import tier data from: `import taxonomy from '../data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json'` — the tiers are in taxonomy.tiers (keyed by T1, T2, T3, T4), each has label, color, bgColor, borderColor, description, riskIfDisclosed, accessDefault

**File 2: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/TierOverview.jsx**

A detailed page explaining each tier:
- Page title "Classification Tiers" with subtitle
- For each tier (T1-T4), show a detailed card with:
  - Tier label and color indicator
  - Description
  - Risk if disclosed
  - Default access level
  - Example data types (pull 4-5 examples from taxonomy.categories for each tier)
  - Handling summary (brief — linking to full handling matrix page)
- Import: `import taxonomy from '../data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json'`
- To get example data types per tier, iterate through taxonomy.categories and collect dataTypes where tier matches

Make sure both files are complete, working React components with proper exports. Use Tailwind utility classes for all styling.

{
  "tiers": {
    "T1": {
      "id": "T1",
      "label": "Public",
      "color": "#16a34a",
      "bgColor": "#f0fdf4",
      "borderColor": "#bbf7d0",
      "description": "Information intended for public consumption. No harm if disclosed; may already be published or required to be publicly available.",
      "riskIfDisclosed": "None",
      "accessDefault": "Anyone"
    },
    "T2": {
      "id": "T2",
      "label": "Internal",
      "color": "#2563eb",
      "bgColor": "#eff6ff",
      "borderColor": "#bfdbfe",
      "description": "Operational information for staff and authorized volunteers. Low harm if accidentally disclosed, but not intended for public release.",
      "riskIfDisclosed": "Low",
      "accessDefault": "All staff with a need"
    },
    "T3": {
      "id": "T3",
      "label": "Confidential",
      "color": "#d97706",
      "bgColor": "#fffbeb",
      "borderColor": "#fde68a",
      "description": "Sensitive organizational or personal data. Moderate-to-significant harm if disclosed, including reputational damage, loss of donor trust, or regulatory concern.",
      "riskIfDisclosed": "Moderate to High",
      "accessDefault": "Role-based, need-to-know"
    },
    "T4": {
      "id": "T4",
      "label": "Restricted",
      "color": "#dc2626",
      "bgColor": "#fef2f2",
      "borderColor": "#fecaca",
      "description": "Highest sensitivity data. Disclosure could cause severe harm including legal liability, regulatory penalties, threat to personal safety, or identity theft.",
      "riskIfDisclosed": "Severe",
      "accessDefault": "Explicit authorization only"
    }
  },
  "categories": [
    {
      "id": "donor-funder",

        {
          "name": "Published donor lists (annual report, honor roll)",
          "tier": "T1",
          "rationale": "Explicitly published with donor consent",
          "regulations": [],
          "sectors": ["all"]
        },
        {
          "name": "Fundraising campaign totals and public goal progress",
          "tier": "T1",
          "rationale": "Aggregate totals shared publicly to encourage giving",
          "regulations": [],
          "sectors": ["all"]
        },
        {
          "name": "Donor name and giving history/totals",
          "tier": "T3",
          "rationale": "Individual giving data is confidential; disclosure could embarrass donors or invite solicitation",
          "regulations": ["STATE_PRIVACY"],
          "sectors": ["all"]
        },
        {
          "name": "Donor contact information (address, phone, email)",
          "tier": "T3",
          "rationale": "Personal contact data requires protection under privacy best practices",
          "regulations": ["STATE_PRIVACY", "GDPR"],
          "sectors": ["all"]
        },
        {
          "name": "Donor payment method, credit card, and ACH/bank data",
          "tier": "T4",
          "rationale": "Financial account data; breach causes direct financial harm and regulatory liability",
          "regulations": ["PCI-DSS", "STATE_PRIVACY"],
          "sectors": ["all"]
        },
        {
          "name": "Wealth screening and capacity scores",
          "tier": "T3",
          "rationale": "Proprietary fundraising intelligence; disclosure damages donor trust and competitive position",
          "regulations": [],
          "sectors": ["all"]
        },
        {
          "name": "Donor communication preferences and opt-outs",
          "tier": "T3",
          "rationale": "Personal preferences linked to identifiable individuals",
          "regulations": ["CAN-SPAM", "GDPR"],
          "sectors": ["all"]
        },
        {
          "name": "Major gift solicitation strategies and cultivation plans",
          "tier": "T3",
          "rationale": "Internal fundraising strategy; disclosure undermines donor relationships",
          "regulations": [],
          "sectors": ["all"]
        },
        {
          "name": "Planned giving and estate/bequest documentation",
          "tier": "T4",
          "rationale": "Legal and financial documents with high sensitivity; involves estate planning and testamentary intent",
          "regulations": ["STATE_PRIVACY"],
          "sectors": ["all"]
        },
        {

          "regulations": ["STATE_PRIVACY"],
          "sectors": ["advocacy"]
        },
        {
          "name": "Lapsed donor reason codes and exit survey data",

        {

tool_input.prompt:
Create two React page components for a nonprofit data classification web app using Tailwind CSS v3 classes.

**File 1: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/DataTypeBrowser.jsx**

A searchable, filterable data type browser:
- Import: `import taxonomy from '../data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json'`
- taxonomy.categories is an array of { id, name, icon, description, dataTypes: [{ name, tier, rationale, regulations, sectors }] }
- taxonomy.tiers is { T1: {...}, T2: {...}, T3: {...}, T4: {...} } with label, color, bgColor, borderColor
- Features:
  - Text search input (filters data types by name, rationale)
  - Category filter (dropdown or pill buttons showing all 10 categories)
  - Tier filter (4 tier buttons, color-coded, toggleable)
  - Results count display
  - Data types shown as rows/cards grouped by category, each showing: name, tier badge (colored), rationale, regulation badges
  - Regulation badges should be small pills (e.g., "HIPAA" in a small colored badge)
  - Expandable/collapsible categories
  - When no filters: show all categories expanded with their data types
- Use useState for search, category filter, tier filter
- Clean, scannable layout — this is a reference tool

**File 2: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/HandlingMatrix.jsx**

An interactive handling controls matrix:
- Import: `import matrixData from '../data/handling-matrix.json'`
- Import: `import taxonomy from '../data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json'` (for tier colors)
- matrixData.controls is an array of { id, category, name, description, tiers: { T1: { requirement, label, detail }, T2: {...}, T3: {...}, T4: {...} } }
- Display as a responsive table/grid:
  - Rows = controls grouped by category
  - Columns = T1, T2, T3, T4 (color-coded headers)
  - Cells show the label with color coding: "none"=green, "optional"=blue, "recommended"=amber, "required"=red, "prohibited"=dark red
  - Clicking a cell expands to show the detail text
- Category group headers to break up the table
- Mobile-responsive: on small screens, show as stacked cards instead of table
- Add a legend at the top explaining the requirement levels

Make sure both files are complete, working React components with proper exports.

tool_input.prompt:
Create two React page components for a nonprofit data classification web app using Tailwind CSS v3 classes.

**File 1: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/WorkspaceGuide.jsx**

Google Workspace implementation guide:
- Import: `import workspaceData from '../data/workspace-features.json'`
- workspaceData.tiers is an array of { id, name, equivalent, pricePerUser, features: { dlp: { available, label, detail }, ... }, recommendations: [...] }
- workspaceData.implementationChecklist has keys like "free", "businessStandard", "businessPlus", "enterprise" — each an array of { step, path, detail }
- Features:
  - Tier selector at top (4 buttons: Free, Business Standard, Business Plus, Enterprise) with prices shown
  - Selected tier shows:
    - Feature availability grid (15 features as rows, with green check / red X / yellow partial indicators)
    - Recommendations list
    - Implementation checklist with expandable steps showing the Admin Console path and details
  - Side-by-side comparison option: show all 4 tiers as columns with features as rows
  - "Upgrade recommendation" callout when on Free tier noting what they're missing
- Clean, practical layout — this is actionable guidance, not a sales pitch

**File 2: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/DecisionTree.jsx**

Interactive "What tier is this?" decision tree:
- NO external imports needed — the tree logic is self-contained
- Build an interactive flowchart where users answer yes/no questions to determine the classification tier for a piece of data
- Decision flow:
  1. "Is this information intended for public consumption?" → Yes = T1 Public (done)
  2. "Does this data identify a specific individual (name, SSN, email, photo, etc.)?" → No = go to step 5
  3. "Is this personal data protected by law or regulation (HIPAA, FERPA, state privacy, etc.)?" → Yes = T4 Restricted
  4. "Could disclosure cause significant harm to an individual (safety, financial, reputational)?" → Yes = T4 Restricted → No = T3 Confidential
  5. "Is this sensitive organizational information (strategy, financials, legal, board executive sessions)?" → Yes = T3 Confidential
  6. "Would disclosure cause embarrassment or competitive disadvantage?" → Yes = T3 Confidential → No = T2 Internal
- UI: Show one question at a time as a card, with Yes/No buttons
- Show progress (which questions were answered and how)
- When a tier is determined, show a result card with the tier name, color, description, and link to the full tier details
- "Start Over" button to reset
- Below the interactive tree, show a static quick-reference version as a simple flowchart diagram (using styled divs and arrows, not an image)
- Use useState to track current question and answer history

Make sure both files are complete, working React components with proper exports.

**`/Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/TierOverview.jsx`**

tool_input.prompt:
Create two React page components for a nonprofit data classification web app. The app uses Tailwind CSS v3 (via @tailwindcss/vite, so use standard Tailwind classes). The design should be clean, warm, accessible — earthy greens and stone colors. Import data from the taxonomy file.

**File 1: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/Landing.jsx**

A landing page that:
- Has a hero section with a gradient background (emerald/teal) explaining what data classification is and why nonprofits need it
- "Protect Your Mission by Protecting Your Data" as the headline
- Subtitle: "A free, practical guide to classifying and handling sensitive information in your nonprofit — built for Google Workspace"
- Shows the 4 tiers as color-coded cards (green=Public, blue=Internal, amber=Confidential, red=Restricted) with brief descriptions
- Has a "Get Started" section with 3 cards linking to: Guide (learn), Decision Tree (classify), Wizard (generate policy)
- Uses Link from react-router-dom for navigation
- Import tier data from: `import taxonomy from '../data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json'` — the tiers are in taxonomy.tiers (keyed by T1, T2, T3, T4), each has label, color, bgColor, borderColor, description, riskIfDisclosed, accessDefault

**File 2: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/TierOverview.jsx**

A detailed page explaining each tier:
- Page title "Classification Tiers" with subtitle
- For each tier (T1-T4), show a detailed card with:
  - Tier label and color indicator
  - Description
  - Risk if disclosed
  - Default access level
  - Example data types (pull 4-5 examples from taxonomy.categories for each tier)
  - Handling summary (brief — linking to full handling matrix page)
- Import: `import taxonomy from '../data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json'`
- To get example data types per tier, iterate through taxonomy.categories and collect dataTypes where tier matches

tool_input.prompt:
Create two React page components for a nonprofit data classification web app using Tailwind CSS v3 classes.

**File 1: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/WorkspaceGuide.jsx**

Google Workspace implementation guide:
- Import: `import workspaceData from '../data/workspace-features.json'`
- workspaceData.tiers is an array of { id, name, equivalent, pricePerUser, features: { dlp: { available, label, detail }, ... }, recommendations: [...] }
- workspaceData.implementationChecklist has keys like "free", "businessStandard", "businessPlus", "enterprise" — each an array of { step, path, detail }
- Features:
  - Tier selector at top (4 buttons: Free, Business Standard, Business Plus, Enterprise) with prices shown
  - Selected tier shows:
    - Feature availability grid (15 features as rows, with green check / red X / yellow partial indicators)
    - Recommendations list
    - Implementation checklist with expandable steps showing the Admin Console path and details
  - Side-by-side comparison option: show all 4 tiers as columns with features as rows
  - "Upgrade recommendation" callout when on Free tier noting what they're missing
- Clean, practical layout — this is actionable guidance, not a sales pitch

**File 2: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/DecisionTree.jsx**

Interactive "What tier is this?" decision tree:
- NO external imports needed — the tree logic is self-contained
- Build an interactive flowchart where users answer yes/no questions to determine the classification tier for a piece of data
- Decision flow:
  1. "Is this information intended for public consumption?" → Yes = T1 Public (done)
  2. "Does this data identify a specific individual (name, SSN, email, photo, etc.)?" → No = go to step 5
  3. "Is this personal data protected by law or regulation (HIPAA, FERPA, state privacy, etc.)?" → Yes = T4 Restricted
  4. "Could disclosure cause significant harm to an individual (safety, financial, reputational)?" → Yes = T4 Restricted → No = T3 Confidential
  5. "Is this sensitive organizational information (strategy, financials, legal, board executive sessions)?" → Yes = T3 Confidential
  6. "Would disclosure cause embarrassment or competitive disadvantage?" → Yes = T3 Confidential → No = T2 Internal
- UI: Show one question at a time as a card, with Yes/No buttons
- Show progress (which questions were answered and how)
- When a tier is determined, show a result card with the tier name, color, description, and link to the full tier details
- "Start Over" button to reset
- Below the interactive tree, show a static quick-reference version as a simple flowchart diagram (using styled divs and arrows, not an image)
- Use useState to track current question and answer history

Make sure both files are complete, working React components with proper exports.

- Shows one question at a time as a card with Yes/No buttons and help text
- Tracks answer history with a visual timeline (numbered steps with yes/no badges)
- Result card shows tier name, color, description, examples, and link to `/guide/tiers`
- Start Over button to reset
- Static flowchart below the interactive section built with styled divs and arrow connectors, showing the full branching logic visually
- Uses `useState` for current question and history tracking

Both components follow the existing project patterns: Tailwind v3 classes, emerald/stone color palette, `react-router-dom` Link components, and the same card/section layout style used in `Landing.jsx`. The build fails only because other pages referenced in `App.jsx` (Wizard, TierOverview, etc.) haven't been created yet.

tool_input.prompt:
Create two files for a nonprofit data classification web app. The wizard is a multi-step form that generates a downloadable Word document.

**File 1: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/Wizard.jsx**

A multi-step policy generation wizard with 6 steps. Uses Tailwind CSS v3 classes. Earthy green/stone palette.

**Step 1: Organization Basics**
- Organization name (text input, required)
- Staff size (select: 1-10, 11-25, 26-50, 51-100, 101-250, 250+)
- Sector (checkboxes, multi-select): General/Human Services, Health, Education, Housing, Advocacy/Policy, Arts & Culture, Environment, Religious
- Primary state of operation (text input — for state privacy law guidance)

**Step 2: Platform**
- Workspace platform (radio: Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Other/Mixed)
- If Google Workspace: tier (radio: Free/Nonprofits, Business Standard, Business Plus, Enterprise Standard)

**Step 3: Data Inventory**
- Import: `import taxonomy from '../data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json'`
- Show all 10 categories as expandable sections
- Each category shows its data types as checkboxes
- Pre-select common data types based on sector selection from step 1:
  - "all" sector types are always pre-selected
  - If "health" selected, pre-select health-sector data types
  - etc.
- User can check/uncheck individual types
- Show a counter of selected types and their tier distribution (e.g., "42 selected: 5 Public, 15 Internal, 16 Confidential, 6 Restricted")

**Step 4: Regulatory Check**
- Based on selected data types from step 3, automatically detect which regulations apply
- Show each applicable regulation as a card with:
  - Regulation name (e.g., "HIPAA — Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act")
  - Plain-language explanation of what it means for them
  - Which of their selected data types trigger it
- If no regulations detected, show a green "No specific regulatory requirements detected" message
- Allow user to manually add regulations they know apply

**Step 5: Review**
- Summary of all selections:
  - Organization info
  - Platform details
  - Data inventory summary by tier (counts)
  - Applicable regulations
  - Handling requirements highlights (most restrictive controls that apply)
- "Edit" buttons to go back to each step

**Step 6: Generate**
- "Generate Your Policy" button
- When clicked, calls generatePolicy() from '../lib/generatePolicy.js'
- Shows a loading state while generating
- Then shows a success state with download button
- Also shows next steps: "Share with your team", "Schedule annual review", "Set up Google Workspace controls"

**UI Pattern:**
- Progress bar at top showing steps 1-6
- Previous/Next buttons at bottom
- Next button disabled until required fields are filled
- Smooth transitions between steps
- All state managed with useState/useReducer

Use useState with an object to track all wizard state:
```js
const [state, setState] = useState({
  orgName: '',
  staffSize: '',
  sectors: [],
  primaryState: '',
  platform: '',
  workspaceTier: '',
  selectedDataTypes: [], // array of { categoryId, dataTypeName, tier }
  additionalRegulations: [],
})
```

**File 2: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/lib/generatePolicy.js**

Word document generator using the `docx` npm package. 

```js
import { Document, Packer, Paragraph, TextRun, Table, TableRow, TableCell, WidthType, BorderStyle, HeadingLevel, AlignmentType, ShadingType } from 'docx'
import { saveAs } from 'file-saver'
import taxonomy from '../data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json'
import matrixData from '../data/handling-matrix.json'
```

The function `generatePolicy(wizardState)` should:

1. Create a beautifully formatted Word document with these sections:

**Cover / Title Section:**
- "[Organization Name]" as title
- "Data Classification & Handling Policy" as subtitle
- "Generated [date]" 
- "Prepared with the Nonprofit Data Classification Guide — nptogether.org"

**Section 1: Purpose & Scope**
- Standard purpose paragraph explaining why data classification matters
- Scope: "This policy applies to all employees, contractors, volunteers, and partners of [org name] who create, access, store, or transmit organizational data."
- Reference to NIST SP 800-60 and CIS Controls v8.1

**Section 2: Classification Tiers**
- For each tier (T1-T4): name, description, risk level, access default
- Formatted as a table with colored headers (use shading — green for T1, blue T2, amber T3, red T4)

**Section 3: Data Inventory**
- Table showing the user's selected data types grouped by category
- Columns: Data Type | Classification Tier | Applicable Regulations
- Only include categories/types that the user selected

**Section 4: Handling Requirements**
- Pull from handling-matrix.json
- Show relevant controls as a table
- Columns: Control | Public | Internal | Confidential | Restricted
- Show requirement labels in each cell

**Section 5: [Platform]-Specific Controls**
- If Google Workspace: specific guidance based on their tier
- List what they CAN do at their tier
- List what they'd gain from upgrading (if not on Enterprise)

**Section 6: Roles & Responsibilities**
- Data Lead: monitors regulations, reviews vendor contracts, evaluates breaches
- Data Governance Team: identifies improvements, contributes to policy development
- Department/Program Heads: ensures team training, promotes security culture
- All Employees: follow policy, report concerns, complete annual acknowledgment
- Contractors/Partners: adhere to policy per agreement terms

**Section 7: Labeling Procedures**
- File naming: "[TIER] - Document Name" for Confidential and Restricted
- Google Drive labels (if on Business Standard+): apply classification label to all files
- Email: add "[CONFIDENTIAL]" or "[RESTRICTED]" to subject line
- Physical documents: mark header/footer with classification level

**Section 8: Incident Reporting**
- What to report: unauthorized access, accidental sharing, lost devices, phishing
- How to report: contact Data Lead immediately
- Response: Data Lead evaluates, escalates if needed, documents

**Section 9: Policy Acknowledgment**
- Annual acknowledgment requirement
- Include acknowledgment statement text

**Section 10: Appendix — Regulatory Requirements**
- List each applicable regulation with a brief description

**Formatting:**
- Use HeadingLevel.HEADING_1 for section titles
- Use HeadingLevel.HEADING_2 for subsections
- Use tables with proper borders and shading
- Font: Calibri 11pt for body, 14pt for headings
- Add page breaks between major sections
- Professional, clean formatting that looks good in both Word and Google Docs

The function should call `Packer.toBlob(doc)` and then `saveAs(blob, filename)` to trigger download.

Filename format: `Data-Classification-Policy-[OrgName]-[YYYY-MM-DD].docx`

Make sure both files are complete and working. The Wizard imports and calls generatePolicy. The generatePolicy function handles all docx creation and download.

) } function Footer() { return (

payload.last_assistant_message:
Build succeeds. Both files are created and working. Here is a summary:

**Created files:**

1. `/Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/Wizard.jsx` -- A 6-step policy wizard with:
   - Step 1: Organization basics (name, staff size, sector checkboxes, primary state)
   - Step 2: Platform selection (Google Workspace with tier sub-selection, Microsoft 365, Other)
   - Step 3: Data inventory with all 10 taxonomy categories as expandable sections, pre-selected based on sector, with a live tier distribution counter
   - Step 4: Regulatory check showing auto-detected regulations as cards with triggering data types, plus manual regulation entry
   - Step 5: Review summary with edit buttons for each section
   - Step 6: Generate button with loading state, success message, and next steps
   - Progress bar, prev/next navigation, and validation throughout

2. `/Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/lib/generatePolicy.js` -- Word document generator producing a 10-section policy:
   - Cover page with org name, title, date, and attribution
   - Purpose & Scope with NIST/CIS references
   - Classification Tiers table with color-coded shading
   - Data Inventory tables grouped by category with tier and regulation columns
   - Handling Requirements matrix (all 15 controls from handling-matrix.json across 4 tiers)
   - Platform-specific controls (Google Workspace tier-aware with available/upgrade guidance, Microsoft 365, or generic)
   - Roles & Responsibilities (5 role categories)
   - Labeling Procedures (file naming, Drive labels for eligible tiers, email, physical)
   - Incident Reporting procedures
   - Policy Acknowledgment form with signature lines
   - Appendix with detailed regulation descriptions

The app was already wired up in `App.jsx` to import and route to the Wizard at `/wizard`. The build completes cleanly.

tool_input.prompt:
Create two files for a nonprofit data classification web app. The wizard is a multi-step form that generates a downloadable Word document.

**File 1: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/pages/Wizard.jsx**

A multi-step policy generation wizard with 6 steps. Uses Tailwind CSS v3 classes. Earthy green/stone palette.

**Step 1: Organization Basics**
- Organization name (text input, required)
- Staff size (select: 1-10, 11-25, 26-50, 51-100, 101-250, 250+)
- Sector (checkboxes, multi-select): General/Human Services, Health, Education, Housing, Advocacy/Policy, Arts & Culture, Environment, Religious
- Primary state of operation (text input — for state privacy law guidance)

**Step 2: Platform**
- Workspace platform (radio: Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Other/Mixed)
- If Google Workspace: tier (radio: Free/Nonprofits, Business Standard, Business Plus, Enterprise Standard)

**Step 3: Data Inventory**
- Import: `import taxonomy from '../data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json'`
- Show all 10 categories as expandable sections
- Each category shows its data types as checkboxes
- Pre-select common data types based on sector selection from step 1:
  - "all" sector types are always pre-selected
  - If "health" selected, pre-select health-sector data types
  - etc.
- User can check/uncheck individual types
- Show a counter of selected types and their tier distribution (e.g., "42 selected: 5 Public, 15 Internal, 16 Confidential, 6 Restricted")

**Step 4: Regulatory Check**
- Based on selected data types from step 3, automatically detect which regulations apply
- Show each applicable regulation as a card with:
  - Regulation name (e.g., "HIPAA — Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act")
  - Plain-language explanation of what it means for them
  - Which of their selected data types trigger it
- If no regulations detected, show a green "No specific regulatory requirements detected" message
- Allow user to manually add regulations they know apply

**Step 5: Review**
- Summary of all selections:
  - Organization info
  - Platform details
  - Data inventory summary by tier (counts)
  - Applicable regulations
  - Handling requirements highlights (most restrictive controls that apply)
- "Edit" buttons to go back to each step

**Step 6: Generate**
- "Generate Your Policy" button
- When clicked, calls generatePolicy() from '../lib/generatePolicy.js'
- Shows a loading state while generating
- Then shows a success state with download button
- Also shows next steps: "Share with your team", "Schedule annual review", "Set up Google Workspace controls"

**UI Pattern:**
- Progress bar at top showing steps 1-6
- Previous/Next buttons at bottom
- Next button disabled until required fields are filled
- Smooth transitions between steps
- All state managed with useState/useReducer

Use useState with an object to track all wizard state:
```js
const [state, setState] = useState({
  orgName: '',
  staffSize: '',
  sectors: [],
  primaryState: '',
  platform: '',
  workspaceTier: '',
  selectedDataTypes: [], // array of { categoryId, dataTypeName, tier }
  additionalRegulations: [],
})
```

**File 2: /Users/joshua/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/lib/generatePolicy.js**

Word document generator using the `docx` npm package. 

```js
import { Document, Packer, Paragraph, TextRun, Table, TableRow, TableCell, WidthType, BorderStyle, HeadingLevel, AlignmentType, ShadingType } from 'docx'
import { saveAs } from 'file-saver'
import taxonomy from '../data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json'
import matrixData from '../data/handling-matrix.json'
```

The function `generatePolicy(wizardState)` should:

1. Create a beautifully formatted Word document with these sections:

**Cover / Title Section:**
- "[Organization Name]" as title
- "Data Classification & Handling Policy" as subtitle
- "Generated [date]" 
- "Prepared with the Nonprofit Data Classification Guide — nptogether.org"

**Section 1: Purpose & Scope**
- Standard purpose paragraph explaining why data classification matters
- Scope: "This policy applies to all employees, contractors, volunteers, and partners of [org name] who create, access, store, or transmit organizational data."
- Reference to NIST SP 800-60 and CIS Controls v8.1

**Section 2: Classification Tiers**
- For each tier (T1-T4): name, description, risk level, access default
- Formatted as a table with colored headers (use shading — green for T1, blue T2, amber T3, red T4)

**Section 3: Data Inventory**
- Table showing the user's selected data types grouped by category
- Columns: Data Type | Classification Tier | Applicable Regulations
- Only include categories/types that the user selected

**Section 4: Handling Requirements**
- Pull from handling-matrix.json
- Show relevant controls as a table
- Columns: Control | Public | Internal | Confidential | Restricted
- Show requirement labels in each cell

**Section 5: [Platform]-Specific Controls**
- If Google Workspace: specific guidance based on their tier
- List what they CAN do at their tier
- List what they'd gain from upgrading (if not on Enterprise)

**Section 6: Roles & Responsibilities**
- Data Lead: monitors regulations, reviews vendor contracts, evaluates breaches
- Data Governance Team: identifies improvements, contributes to policy development
- Department/Program Heads: ensures team training, promotes security culture

- Contractors/Partners: adhere to policy per agreement terms

- Email: add "[CONFIDENTIAL]" or "[RESTRICTED]" to subject line
- Physical documents: mark header/footer with classification level

**Section 8: Incident Reporting**
- What to report: unauthorized access, accidental sharing, lost devices, phishing
- How to report: contact Data Lead immediately
- Response: Data Lead evaluates, escalates if needed, documents

**Section 9: Policy Acknowledgment**
- Annual acknowledgment requirement
- Include acknowledgment statement text

**Section 10: Appendix — Regulatory Requirements**
- List each applicable regulation with a brief description

- Use HeadingLevel.HEADING_1 for section titles
- Use HeadingLevel.HEADING_2 for subsections
- Use tables with proper borders and shading
- Font: Calibri 11pt for body, 14pt for headings
- Add page breaks between major sections
- Professional, clean formatting that looks good in both Word and Google Docs

**Step 1: Organization Basics**
- Organization name (text input, required)
- Staff size (select: 1-10, 11-25, 26-50, 51-100, 101-250, 250+)
- Sector (checkboxes, multi-select): General/Human Services, Health, Education, Housing, Advocacy/Policy, Arts & Culture, Environment, Religious
- Primary state of operation (text input — for state privacy law guidance)

**Step 2: Platform**
- Workspace platform (radio: Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Other/Mixed)

  - etc.

**Step 4: Regulatory Check**

**Step 5: Review**

  - Applicable regulations

tool_input.content:
# DataClassification — Data Classification Policy Generator

Generating tailored data classification and handling policies for nonprofit vCISO clients. USE WHEN creating data classification policies, onboarding new vCISO clients needing data governance, or when a client asks about classifying their data. NOT for: general security assessments (use SecurityAssessment), compliance tracking (use ComplianceTracking), or third-party risk (use TPRM).

## Desired Outcome

A complete, professionally formatted data classification and handling policy (.docx) tailored to the client's sector, platform, regulatory environment, and data types — ready for review and adoption.

## Inputs

Gather these via interview or from existing client knowledge:

| Input | Required | Source |
|-------|----------|--------|
| Organization name | Yes | Client |
| Workspace platform (Google Workspace / M365) | Yes | Client or admin console |
| Google Workspace tier (Free / Standard / Plus / Enterprise) | If GWS | Admin console |
| Staff count | Yes | Client |
| Sector tags | Yes | Interview (health, education, housing, advocacy, arts, human_services, environment, general) |
| Regulatory environment | Yes | Interview + inference from sector |
| Special sensitive data types | Recommended | Interview (DV clients, substance use, immigration, minors) |
| Primary state of operation | Recommended | For state privacy law applicability |

## Interview Flow

If inputs are not provided, conduct a brief interview:

1. "What's the organization's name and approximately how many staff?"
2. "What sector(s) do you operate in?" (health, education, housing, advocacy, arts, human services, environment)
3. "Are you on Google Workspace or Microsoft 365? If Google Workspace, do you know your tier?"
4. "Do you handle any of these sensitive data types: health/medical records, substance use records, domestic violence client data, immigration status, data on minors, criminal records?"
5. "What state is your primary operation in?"

## Classification Framework

**4 Tiers:**

| Tier | Label | Risk if Disclosed | Access Default |
|------|-------|-------------------|----------------|
| T1 | Public | None | Anyone |
| T2 | Internal | Low | All staff with a need |
| T3 | Confidential | Moderate to High | Role-based, need-to-know |
| T4 | Restricted | Severe | Explicit authorization only |

**Key principle:** Internal (T2) is the default classification. Unclassified data defaults to Internal, not Public. This prevents data from falling through the cracks.

## Data Taxonomy Reference

The complete nonprofit data taxonomy lives in the web app repo:
`~/Projects/nonprofit-data-classification/src/data/nonprofit-data-taxonomy.json`

129 data types across 10 categories:
1. Donor & Funder Data
2. Program Participant / Client / Beneficiary Data
3. Employee & HR Data
4. Financial Data
5. Legal & Governance Data
6. Communications & Advocacy Data
7. Grant & Proposal Data
8. Volunteer Data
9. Case Management Data
10. Research Data

## Policy Document Structure

Generate a Word document (.docx) using the mtm-standard docx-js generator with these sections:

1. **Cover Page** — Org name, "Data Classification & Handling Policy", date, MTM attribution
2. **Purpose & Scope** — Why classification matters, who it applies to, NIST/CIS references
3. **Classification Tiers** — 4-tier definitions with color-coded table
4. **Data Inventory** — Client's data types grouped by category with tier and regulation columns (filtered by their sector)
5. **Handling Requirements** — Matrix from `handling-matrix.json`: NDA, encryption, access control, AI tools, mobile, email, disposal, labeling, printing, cloud storage, third-party sharing
6. **Platform-Specific Controls** — Google Workspace or M365 guidance matched to their tier, with upgrade recommendations
7. **Roles & Responsibilities** — Data Lead, Data Governance Team, Department Heads, All Employees, Contractors/Partners
8. **Labeling Procedures** — File naming conventions, Drive labels (if available), email subject prefixes, physical document marking
9. **Incident Reporting** — What to report, how to report, response process
10. **Policy Acknowledgment** — Annual requirement, acknowledgment statement text
11. **Appendix: Regulatory Requirements** — Applicable regulations with plain-language descriptions

## Generation Process

1. Read the taxonomy JSON to get sector-relevant data types
2. Read the handling matrix JSON for controls
3. Read workspace features JSON if Google Workspace
4. Generate the .docx using the Docx skill's mtm-standard-doc.js component library
5. Save to `~/Desktop/Vishali/` with filename: `Data-Classification-Policy-[OrgName]-[YYYY-MM-DD].docx`
6. Draft delivery email if requested

## Regulatory Quick Reference

| Regulation | Triggered By | Key Requirement |
|------------|-------------|-----------------|
| HIPAA | Health/medical records | PHI must be T4; minimum necessary; BAAs required |
| FERPA | Education records | Student records T4; parental consent for minors |
| HMIS | Homeless services data | HUD PPI requirements; T4 classification |
| PCI-DSS | Payment card data | Card data T4; no storage of CVV; encryption required |
| GDPR | EU donors/beneficiaries/staff | Consent tracking; right to erasure; DPA required |
| 42 CFR Part 2 | Substance use records | Stricter than HIPAA; specific re-disclosure prohibitions |
| VAWA | DV client data | Address confidentiality; safety-critical |
| COPPA | Data on children under 13 | Parental consent required; T4 |
| STATE_PRIVACY | CO, NJ, DE, MN (no nonprofit exemption) | Donor/constituent data creates direct obligations |

## Special Advisories

- **42 CFR Part 2** orgs need explicit legal guidance — this regulation is stricter than HIPAA
- **Colorado and New Jersey** do NOT exempt nonprofits from state privacy law — flag for any client in those states
- **501(c)(4) donor identity** (Schedule B) is IRS-confidential and always T4
- **DV client addresses** are safety-critical and always T4 regardless of other factors

## Quality Checks (ISC)

Before delivering:
- [ ] Policy uses client's actual org name throughout (no placeholders left)
- [ ] Data types are filtered to their sector (no irrelevant categories)
- [ ] Platform guidance matches their actual tier (not generic)
- [ ] All applicable regulations are listed with plain-language explanations
- [ ] Handling matrix includes the AI tools row
- [ ] Roles section uses nonprofit-appropriate titles (not corporate)

## Web App Reference

The interactive version of this tool is deployed at the nonprofit data classification web app. Clients can:
- Browse the full data taxonomy
- Use the decision tree to classify specific data
- Generate their own starter policy via the wizard
- Review Google Workspace implementation guidance

## Related Skills

- **SecurityAssessment** — Full vCISO security assessment (data classification is one component)
- **TPRM** — Third-party risk assessment (vendor data handling evaluation)
- **ComplianceTracking** — Ongoing compliance monitoring across frameworks
- **VantaCoach** — Vanta platform configuration including data classification controls

10. **Policy Acknowledgment** — Annual requirement, acknowledgment statement text
11. **Appendix: Regulatory Requirements** — Applicable regulations with plain-language descriptions

## Generation Process

1. Read the taxonomy JSON to get sector-relevant data types
2. Read the handling matrix JSON for controls
3. Read workspace features JSON if Google Workspace
4. Generate the .docx using the Docx skill's mtm-standard-doc.js component library
5. Save to `~/Desktop/Vishali/` with filename: `Data-Classification-Policy-[OrgName]-[YYYY-MM-DD].docx`
6. Draft delivery email if requested

## Regulatory Quick Reference

| Regulation | Triggered By | Key Requirement |
|------------|-------------|-----------------|
| HIPAA | Health/medical records | PHI must be T4; minimum necessary; BAAs required |
| FERPA | Education records | Student records T4; parental consent for minors |
| HMIS | Homeless services data | HUD PPI requirements; T4 classification |
| PCI-DSS | Payment card data | Card data T4; no storage of CVV; encryption required |
| GDPR | EU donors/beneficiaries/staff | Consent tracking; right to erasure; DPA required |
| 42 CFR Part 2 | Substance use records | Stricter than HIPAA; specific re-disclosure prohibitions |
| VAWA | DV client data | Address confidentiality; safety-critical |
| COPPA | Data on children under 13 | Parental consent required; T4 |
| STATE_PRIVACY | CO, NJ, DE, MN (no nonprofit exemption) | Donor/constituent data creates direct obligations |

## Special Advisories

- **42 CFR Part 2** orgs need explicit legal guidance — this regulation is stricter than HIPAA
- **Colorado and New Jersey** do NOT exempt nonprofits from state privacy law — flag for any client in those states
- **501(c)(4) donor identity** (Schedule B) is IRS-confidential and always T4
- **DV client addresses** are safety-critical and always T4 regardless of other factors

## Quality Checks (ISC)

Before delivering:
- [ ] Policy uses client's actual org name throughout (no placeholders left)
- [ ] Data types are filtered to their sector (no irrelevant categories)
- [ ] Platform guidance matches their actual tier (not generic)
- [ ] All applicable regulations are listed with plain-language explanations
- [ ] Handling matrix includes the AI tools row
- [ ] Roles section uses nonprofit-appropriate titles (not corporate)

## Web App Reference

The interactive version of this tool is deployed at the nonprofit data classification web app. Clients can:
- Browse the full data taxonomy
- Use the decision tree to classify specific data
- Generate their own starter policy via the wizard
- Review Google Workspace implementation guidance

## Related Skills

- **SecurityAssessment** — Full vCISO security assessment (data classification is one component)
- **TPRM** — Third-party risk assessment (vendor data handling evaluation)
- **ComplianceTracking** — Ongoing compliance monitoring across frameworks
- **VantaCoach** — Vanta platform configuration including data classification controls
